business_goals: Develop a new decentralized architecture to replace the traditional centralized server model
Achieve flexibility and openness to cope with highly dynamic warehouse environments
Evaluate the feasibility and tradeoffs of Multi-Agent Systems in an industrial setting
technical_constraints: Must operate within the bandwidth limits of a shared 11Mbps wireless network
Must integrate with existing low-level Vehicle Control software
Must utilize legacy libraries for logging, persistence, and security

phase_1_discovery.initial_documentation_status: The architectural documentation was not well structured; the process view was missing, and the deployment view was lacking. Quality attributes were only specified at a high level, and architectural decisions involving tradeoffs were made on an ad-hoc basis.
evaluator_inquiry_questions: How are the process views and deployment views defined in the architecture?
How does the implementation adhere to the strict layering defined in the architectural views?
What are the concrete quality attribute scenarios driving the flexibility and openness requirements?

hardware_view_description: 12 AGV machines equipped with low-level Vehicle Control software
Warehouse layout measuring 75 by 55 meters with distinct zones
runtime_constraints: 11Mbps Wireless Ethernet (IEEE 802.11) shared with Warehouse Management System
AGVs rely on battery power and must periodically charge
Physical path constraints (magnets/cables) limit AGV diversion capabilities

questions: Does the communication overhead of the dynamic CNET protocol exceed the wireless bandwidth limits?
How does the system maintain transport assignment efficiency when communication is limited?
Is the layering of the internal AGV control system strictly enforced in the implementation?

raw_candidate_scenarios.description: AGVs must route efficiently through the layout of the warehouse when executing their transports.
AGVs must not collide; they cannot cross the same intersection at the same moment.
The system must ensure that AGVs do not find themselves in a deadlock situation.
If an operator removes or adds an AGV in a controlled way, the rest of the system continues working.
If a transport has not been picked, the system dynamically changes that transport's assignment to the most suitable AGV.

architectural_approaches.description: A decentralized coordination architecture where each AGV is represented by an autonomous agent that makes local decisions to achieve global warehouse objectives.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Enables individual AGVs to adapt to dynamic warehouse environments and local obstacles without waiting for central server instructions.
The modular nature of agents allows for the system to be extended or modified with new AGVs or behaviors without central reconfiguration.

architectural_approaches.description: Agents communicate directly with one another over the 11Mbps wireless network to coordinate tasks and avoid collisions.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Eliminates the single point of failure and potential bottleneck of a central server, though message volume must be optimized for the 11Mbps limit.

architectural_approaches.description: Structural pattern used to encapsulate legacy Vehicle Control software and existing libraries (logging, security, persistence) to expose a clean interface to the MAS agents.

addressed_attributes.rationale: Decouples the high-level coordination logic from the specific low-level legacy implementations, allowing for easier updates to the MAS logic.
Ensures efficient, low-latency communication between the new agent logic and the existing low-level vehicle control code.

children.scenario_description: Under normal operations, an AGV must coordinate collision avoidance with neighbors via P2P within 200ms.
A new AGV is added to the fleet; it must be discovered and integrated into the MAS coordination logic within 5 minutes without restarting the system.
The legacy logging component is replaced with a new implementation; the Wrapper/Adapter must ensure no changes are required to the agent logic.

scenarios.scenario_text: Under normal operations, an AGV must coordinate collision avoidance with neighbors via P2P within 200ms.
A new AGV is added to the fleet; it must be discovered and integrated into the MAS coordination logic within 5 minutes without restarting the system.
The legacy logging component is replaced with a new implementation; the Wrapper/Adapter must ensure no changes are required to the agent logic.

scenario_reference.text: Under normal operations, an AGV must coordinate collision avoidance with neighbors via P2P within 200ms.

analysis_records.stimulus: AGVs approaching a potential collision point
analysis_records.reasoning: By utilizing P2P communication, the system avoids the latency of a central server round-trip. However, since all AGVs share an 11Mbps wireless medium, the collision avoidance logic is highly dependent on the total message volume and network contention.

risks.description: Network congestion on the 11Mbps shared wireless medium.
risks.rationale: As fleet size increases, the aggregate P2P coordination traffic may exceed the 11Mbps limit, leading to packet collisions and increased latency beyond the 200ms threshold.

non_risks.rationale: The absence of a central coordination server ensures that the failure of one node does not halt the entire fleet's collision avoidance capability.

sensitivity_points.parameter: Number of AGVs in a single coordination zone

scenario_reference.text: A new AGV is added to the fleet; it must be discovered and integrated into the MAS coordination logic within 5 minutes without restarting the system.

analysis_records.stimulus: Addition of a new AGV node to the network
analysis_records.reasoning: The MAS style allows agents to act as autonomous entities. A decentralized discovery process allows the system to remain operational while new nodes join, fulfilling the flexibility requirement.

risks.rationale: If discovery protocols rely on frequent broadcasting, the addition of multiple AGVs simultaneously could saturate the 11Mbps bandwidth.

non_risks.rationale: The decentralized nature of MAS ensures that the existing fleet does not need to pause or restart to recognize a new peer.

scenario_reference.text: The legacy logging component is replaced with a new implementation; the Wrapper/Adapter must ensure no changes are required to the agent logic.

analysis_records.stimulus: Replacement of legacy C++ logging library
analysis_records.response_measure: Zero changes to MAS agent coordination logic
analysis_records.reasoning: The Wrapper/Adapter pattern provides a stable interface for the agents, shielding them from changes in the underlying legacy libraries or third-party implementations.

risks.rationale: If the new logging implementation lacks parity with the legacy interface, the adapter may become overly complex, potentially introducing bugs into the agent-to-legacy communication path.

non_risks.rationale: The use of an adapter ensures that high-level coordination logic is independent of the low-level data persistence or logging formats.
